title: Check if number is square-free
description: Determine if no prime factor appears with exponent >= 2

examples.explanation: n=30, sqrt(30)≈5.47, floor=5. Check d=2: 30%2=0, check 30%(2*2)=30%4=2≠0 → continue. d=3: 30%3=0, check 30%(3*3)=30%9=3≠0 → continue. d=4: 30%4=2≠0 → continue. d=5: 30%5=0, check 30%(5*5)=30%25=5≠0 → continue. Loop ends, return true.
n=18, sqrt(18)≈4.24, floor=4. Check d=2: 18%2=0, check 18%(2*2)=18%4=2≠0 → continue. d=3: 18%3=0, check 18%(3*3)=18%9=0 → found d=3 where d² divides n, return false.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(sqrt(n))
approaches.summary: Iterate through all integers from 2 to sqrt(n), checking if any divides n more than once. If a divisor d divides n, check if d² also divides n. This directly tests for repeated prime factors.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check if Number is Square-Free Using Brute Force
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to determine if a number has no prime factor with exponent ≥ 2 by checking divisors up to sqrt(n).
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that if a number is square-free, no prime factor should appear more than once in its prime factorization. Instead of performing full prime factorization, we can directly test for repeated factors by checking all potential divisors from 2 to sqrt(n). For each divisor d that divides n, we check if d² also divides n. If d² divides n, then d appears at least twice in the factorization, making the number not square-free. This works because any repeated prime factor p must satisfy p ≤ sqrt(n) (since if p > sqrt(n), then p² > n, so it couldn't divide n). The algorithm iterates through integers from 2 to floor(sqrt(n)). For each d, if n % d == 0, check if n % (d*d) == 0. If true, return false (not square-free). Also handle the case where d divides n but d*d might overflow by checking if n % (d*d) == 0 carefully (using integer division). After the loop, return true. Time complexity is O(sqrt(n)) as we check up to sqrt(n) divisors. Space complexity is O(1) using only a few variables. Connection to L1: This uses the key subroutine of checking divisors and their squares. Common mistakes include not handling n ≤ 1 correctly (1 is square-free by definition, numbers ≤ 0 are not square-free), integer overflow when computing d*d (use n/d >= d to check), and missing the case where n itself is a perfect square of a prime. Real code pattern: def is_square_free(n): if n <= 1: return n == 1; for d in range(2, int(sqrt(n))+1): if n % d == 0: if n % (d*d) == 0: return False; return True.

examples.trace: n=30, sqrt(30)≈5.47, floor=5. Check d=2: 30%2=0, check 30%(2*2)=30%4=2≠0 → continue. d=3: 30%3=0, check 30%(3*3)=30%9=3≠0 → continue. d=4: 30%4=2≠0 → continue. d=5: 30%5=0, check 30%(5*5)=30%25=5≠0 → continue. Loop ends, return true.
n=18, sqrt(18)≈4.24, floor=4. Check d=2: 18%2=0, check 18%(2*2)=18%4=2≠0 → continue. d=3: 18%3=0, check 18%(3*3)=18%9=0 → found d=3 where d² divides n, return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Check Divisor and Its Square for Repeated Factors
ladders.desc: Given n and a divisor d, determine if d appears at least twice in n's factorization by checking if d² divides n.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that if a prime factor d divides n more than once, then d² must also divide n. This subroutine is the core of the brute force approach, allowing us to detect repeated factors without full factorization. For a given n and d, first check if d divides n (i.e., n % d == 0). If it does, then check if d² divides n (i.e., n % (d*d) == 0). If both conditions hold, d is a repeated factor. This works because in prime factorization, if d appears with exponent ≥2, then d² is a factor. The algorithm: function check_repeated_factor(n, d): if n % d != 0: return false (d not a factor at all); else: return (n % (d*d) == 0). Time complexity is O(1) per check. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each d in the main loop to test for square-freeness. Connection to L2: This relies on the core logic that repeated factors imply divisibility by the square. Common mistakes include integer overflow when computing d*d for large d (use n/d >= d to check without multiplication), and not handling d=0 or d=1 (but in context, d starts from 2). Real code pattern: def has_repeated_factor(n, d): if n % d != 0: return False; return n % (d*d) == 0; // or check (n//d) % d == 0 to avoid overflow.

examples.trace: Check if 3 divides 18: 18%3=0 → yes. Check if 3²=9 divides 18: 18%9=0 → yes. Return true.
Check if 2 divides 30: 30%2=0 → yes. Check if 2²=4 divides 30: 30%4=2≠0 → no. Return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Repeated Prime Factors Imply Divisibility by Square
ladders.desc: Understand that if a prime factor appears multiple times, its square must divide the number.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is based on prime factorization: any integer n > 1 can be uniquely expressed as a product of primes, e.g., n = p1^e1 * p2^e2 * ... * pk^ek. If a prime factor p has exponent e ≥ 2, then p² is a factor of n because n = p² * (p^(e-2) * other primes). Conversely, if p² divides n, then p must appear at least twice. This core logic allows us to test for square-freeness without computing full exponents: just check for any d such that d² divides n. The algorithm concept: to check if n is square-free, iterate possible d and test if d² divides n. This reduces the problem from exponent checking to divisibility testing. Time complexity depends on how many d we check; in brute force, we check up to sqrt(n). Space is O(1). Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the check_repeated_factor subroutine. Connection to L3: It builds on the building block of prime factorization. Common mistakes include confusing this with checking all squares (e.g., d=4 for n=12, but 4 is not prime; however, if 4 divides n, then 2 is a repeated factor, so it still works because we check all d, not just primes). Real code pattern: The idea is used in loops: for d from 2 to sqrt(n): if n % (d*d) == 0: return False.

examples.trace: Prime factorization: 12 = 2² * 3. Prime 2 has exponent 2, so 2²=4 divides 12. Check: 12%4=0 → not square-free.
Prime factorization: 30 = 2 * 3 * 5. All exponents are 1. For any prime p in {2,3,5}, p² does not divide 30 (4,9,25 do not divide 30). So square-free.

ladders.title: Building Block — Prime Factorization and Exponent Checking
ladders.desc: Decompose a number into its prime factors and check if any exponent is ≥ 2.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that square-freeness can be determined by examining the exponents in the prime factorization. If all exponents are 1, the number is square-free; if any exponent ≥ 2, it is not. This building block involves finding all prime factors of n and their exponents. The algorithm: initialize an empty list for factors. While n > 1, find the smallest prime divisor d (starting from 2), count how many times d divides n (exponent), and record d and exponent. Divide n by d^exponent. Repeat with next d. After factorization, check all exponents: if any exponent ≥ 2, return false; else true. Time complexity: O(sqrt(n)) for factorization (similar to brute force). Space: O(k) for storing factors, where k is number of distinct primes. Connection to L2: This is a more general method that the core logic simplifies; instead of checking exponents, we check squares directly. Connection to L4: It uses basic operations like division and modulo. Common mistakes include inefficient factorization (checking all numbers up to n instead of sqrt(n)), not handling repeated factors correctly in the loop, and missing edge cases like n=1. Real code pattern: def prime_factors(n): factors = []; d=2; while d*d <= n: if n%d==0: exp=0; while n%d==0: n//=d; exp+=1; factors.append((d,exp)); d+=1; if n>1: factors.append((n,1)); return factors. Then check exponents.

examples.trace: Factorize 18: start d=2, 18%2=0 → exp=1, n=9; d=3, 9%3=0 → exp=2, n=1. Factors: (2,1), (3,2). Exponent for 3 is 2 ≥2 → not square-free.
Factorize 30: d=2, 30%2=0 → exp=1, n=15; d=3, 15%3=0 → exp=1, n=5; d=5, 5%5=0 → exp=1, n=1. Factors: (2,1), (3,1), (5,1). All exponents 1 → square-free.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Check Divisibility and Compute Remainder
ladders.desc: Determine if one integer divides another and find the remainder.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that divisibility is fundamental to number theory: a divides b if there exists an integer k such that b = a * k. In computation, we check this using the modulo operator: b % a == 0. The remainder b % a gives the leftover when b is divided by a. This basic operation is used throughout the square-free checking algorithm to test if d divides n and if d² divides n. The algorithm: given two integers a and b, to check if a divides b, compute b % a; if result is 0, then a divides b; else, it does not. To avoid overflow when a is large, use integer division: b // a * a == b. Time complexity is O(1) per operation. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used repeatedly in prime factorization to divide n by d. Connection to L5: It builds on arithmetic operations. Common mistakes include using floating-point division instead of integer modulo, not handling division by zero, and misunderstanding modulo for negative numbers (but in this problem, n is positive). Real code pattern: def divides(a, b): return b % a == 0; or if a != 0: return b % a == 0.

examples.trace: Compute 12 % 3 = 0. Since remainder is 0, 3 divides 12.
Compute 12 % 5 = 2. Since remainder is not 0, 5 does not divide 12.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Integers, Factors, and Prime Numbers
ladders.desc: Understand basic number concepts: integers, divisibility, factors, and prime numbers.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that square-freeness is defined in terms of prime factors, so we need foundational knowledge of integers and primes. An integer is a whole number (positive, negative, or zero; but in this problem, we consider positive integers). A factor (or divisor) of an integer n is an integer d such that n divided by d leaves no remainder (i.e., n % d == 0). For example, factors of 6 are 1,2,3,6. A prime number is an integer greater than 1 that has exactly two factors: 1 and itself (e.g., 2,3,5,7). Every integer greater than 1 can be expressed uniquely as a product of prime numbers (prime factorization). For example, 12 = 2 * 2 * 3 = 2² * 3. This foundation is essential because square-freeness checks if any prime factor appears more than once. The algorithm concept: to work with square-freeness, one must understand how to identify factors and primes. Time and space are not applicable here as it's conceptual. Connection to L4: This foundation enables the basic operation of checking divisibility. Common mistakes include confusing factors with multiples, or not knowing prime definitions. Real code pattern: No code, but understanding: def is_prime(n): if n<2: return False; for i in range(2, int(sqrt(n))+1): if n%i==0: return False; return True.

examples.trace: 6 can be divided by 1,2,3,6 without remainder. Prime factors are primes that divide 6: 2 and 3.
7 is prime, so its only factors are 1 and 7. Prime factor is 7 itself.

approaches.name: Prime Factorization O(sqrt(n))
approaches.summary: Fully factorize n into its prime factors by trial division. Check if any prime exponent is >= 2. This explicitly reveals all prime factors and their multiplicities.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check if Number is Square-Free via Prime Factorization
ladders.desc: Complete solution using trial division to factorize n and check for repeated prime factors.
ladders.explanation: The problem requires determining if a given integer n has no prime factor with exponent ≥ 2, meaning no prime squared divides n. The intuition is that if we fully factorize n into its prime factors, we can directly inspect the multiplicities. The trial division approach works by iterating through potential divisors from 2 up to √n. For each divisor d, we repeatedly divide n by d while d divides n, counting how many times it divides. If the count reaches 2 or more for any d, n is not square-free. After exhausting divisors up to √n, if n > 1, it's a prime factor with exponent 1. The algorithm is O(√n) time because we check divisors up to √n, and O(log n) space for storing counts. This feeds into the parent problem by providing a complete, efficient solution. Common mistakes include not handling n ≤ 1 correctly (1 is square-free by convention, negatives might be handled by absolute value), or stopping the loop at n instead of √n, which degrades to O(n). The pseudocode: function isSquareFree(n): if n ≤ 1: return True; for d from 2 to √n: count = 0; while n % d == 0: n /= d; count += 1; if count ≥ 2: return False; if n > 1: prime factor with exponent 1; return True.

examples.trace: n = 18. d = 2: 18 % 2 == 0, so divide: n = 9, count = 1. 9 % 2 != 0, stop. count = 1 < 2. d = 3: 9 % 3 == 0, divide: n = 3, count = 1. 3 % 3 == 0, divide: n = 1, count = 2. Since count = 2 ≥ 2, return False. 18 = 2 * 3^2, so not square-free.

n = 30. d = 2: 30 % 2 == 0, divide: n = 15, count = 1. 15 % 2 != 0, stop. count = 1. d = 3: 15 % 3 == 0, divide: n = 5, count = 1. 5 % 3 != 0, stop. count = 1. d = 4: skip as 4 > √5 ≈ 2.23. n = 5 > 1, prime factor with exponent 1. All counts < 2, return True. 30 = 2 * 3 * 5, square-free.

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Prime Factorization via Trial Division
ladders.desc: Factorize n into prime factors with their exponents using trial division up to √n.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the full problem: it decomposes n into its prime factors and their multiplicities. The intuition is that any composite number n has a prime factor ≤ √n, so by checking divisors from 2 upward, we can extract all prime factors. The algorithm iterates d from 2 to √n. For each d, if d divides n, it's a prime factor (since we divide out all smaller primes first). We count how many times d divides n by repeatedly dividing n by d while n % d == 0, incrementing a counter. Store the factor and count. After the loop, if n > 1, it's a remaining prime factor with exponent 1. Time is O(√n) as we check up to √n divisors, and space is O(log n) for storing factors. This feeds into L0 by providing the factorization needed to check exponents. Common mistakes include not updating n during division, leading to infinite loops, or not handling the remaining n > 1. Pseudocode: function factorize(n): factors = []; for d from 2 to √n: if n % d == 0: count = 0; while n % d == 0: n /= d; count += 1; factors.append((d, count)); if n > 1: factors.append((n, 1)); return factors.

examples.trace: n = 18. d = 2: 18 % 2 == 0, so while loop: divide n = 9, count = 1; 9 % 2 != 0, stop. Append (2,1). d = 3: 9 % 3 == 0, while: divide n = 3, count = 1; divide n = 1, count = 2; append (3,2). n = 1, done. Factors: 2^1 * 3^2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Checking for Repeated Prime Factors During Factorization
ladders.desc: While factorizing, immediately detect if any prime exponent reaches 2 or more.
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight: instead of fully factorizing and then checking exponents, we can abort early if any exponent ≥ 2 is found, making the algorithm more efficient for non-square-free numbers. The intuition is that as soon as we see a prime dividing n twice, we know n is not square-free, so we can return False immediately. During trial division, for each divisor d, we count how many times it divides n. If count reaches 2, we stop and return False. If we finish the loop without finding such a prime, and n > 1 is a prime with exponent 1, return True. This reduces time in worst case to O(√n) but can be faster. It connects to L1 by optimizing the factorization process. Common mistakes include not resetting count for each d, or incorrectly handling the remaining n. Pseudocode: function checkSquareFreeDuringFactor(n): if n ≤ 1: return True; for d from 2 to √n: count = 0; while n % d == 0: n /= d; count += 1; if count ≥ 2: return False; if n > 1: exponent 1; return True.

examples.trace: n = 18. d = 2: while 18 % 2 == 0: n = 9, count = 1; count = 1 < 2. d = 3: while 9 % 3 == 0: n = 3, count = 1; while 3 % 3 == 0: n = 1, count = 2; count = 2 ≥ 2, return False immediately.
n = 30. d = 2: while 30 % 2 == 0: n = 15, count = 1; count = 1. d = 3: while 15 % 3 == 0: n = 5, count = 1; count = 1. d = 4: skip. n = 5 > 1, exponent 1. All counts < 2, return True.

ladders.title: Building Block — Trial Division to Find Prime Factors
ladders.desc: Iterate through numbers up to √n to find divisors that are prime factors.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves using trial division to identify prime factors of n. The intuition is that if n is composite, it must have a prime factor ≤ √n, so by testing divisors from 2 upward, we can find all prime factors. The algorithm checks each integer d from 2 to √n. If d divides n, then d is a prime factor because any smaller factors would have been divided out earlier. We then divide n by d repeatedly to remove that factor. This is efficient for factorization. Time is O(√n) in worst case, space O(1). It connects to L2 by providing the mechanism to extract factors and check exponents. Common mistakes include not limiting the loop to √n (which makes it O(n)), or not updating n correctly. Pseudocode: function trialDivisionFactors(n): factors = []; for d from 2 to √n: if n % d == 0: while n % d == 0: n /= d; factors.append(d); if n > 1: factors.append(n); return factors.

examples.trace: n = 18. d = 2: 18 % 2 == 0, so while: divide n = 9, append 2; 9 % 2 != 0, stop. d = 3: 9 % 3 == 0, while: divide n = 3, append 3; divide n = 1, append 3; n = 1, done. Factors list: [2, 3, 3].
n = 30. d = 2: 30 % 2 == 0, while: n = 15, append 2; stop. d = 3: 15 % 3 == 0, while: n = 5, append 3; stop. d = 4: skip. n = 5 > 1, append 5. Factors: [2, 3, 5].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Checking Divisibility and Division
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations: test if a divides b, and divide b by a.
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental operation used throughout the approach: checking if an integer a divides another integer b (i.e., b % a == 0), and performing integer division b = b / a. The intuition is that in factorization, we need to repeatedly test if a potential divisor d divides the current n, and if so, reduce n by dividing by d. The algorithm involves using the modulo operator (%) to check divisibility: if n % d == 0, then d divides n. Then, we update n by integer division: n = n // d. This is done in a loop to extract all powers of d. Time per operation is O(1), space O(1). It connects to L3 by enabling the trial division process. Common mistakes include using floating-point division instead of integer division, or not updating n in place. Pseudocode: while n % d == 0: n = n // d; count += 1.

examples.trace: Start: n = 18, d = 2. Check 18 % 2 == 0, true. Enter loop: n = 18 // 2 = 9, count = 1. Check 9 % 2 == 0, false, exit loop. Result: n = 9, count = 1.

Start: n = 27, d = 3. Check 27 % 3 == 0, true. Loop: n = 9, count = 1; check 9 % 3 == 0, true: n = 3, count = 2; check 3 % 3 == 0, true: n = 1, count = 3; check 1 % 3 == 0, false, exit. Result: n = 1, count = 3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Numbers and Factorization
ladders.desc: Understanding prime numbers and how numbers are composed of prime factors.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: understanding that every integer greater than 1 can be uniquely expressed as a product of prime numbers raised to powers, known as its prime factorization. For example, 12 = 2^2 * 3^1. A prime number is a natural number greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. The intuition is that primes are the building blocks of all numbers. In the context of square-free numbers, if any prime exponent in the factorization is 2 or more, the number is not square-free (e.g., 4 = 2^2 is not square-free). This foundational concept connects to L4 by explaining why we check divisibility by primes. Common mistakes include confusing prime with composite numbers, or not understanding uniqueness of factorization. No complex algorithm here, just conceptual understanding. Example: 18 = 2 * 3^2, so it has prime factors 2 and 3, with exponent 2 for 3, thus not square-free.

examples.trace: 12 can be divided by 2: 12 ÷ 2 = 6. 6 ÷ 2 = 3. 3 is prime. So, 12 = 2 * 2 * 3 = 2^2 * 3^1. The exponents are 2 for prime 2 and 1 for prime 3.

examples.input: Is 15 square-free based on its prime factors?
examples.trace: 15 = 3 * 5. The prime factors are 3 and 5, both with exponent 1. Since no exponent is 2 or more, 15 is square-free.

approaches.name: Square Root Method O(sqrt(n))
approaches.summary: Check if n is divisible by any perfect square up to sqrt(n). For each integer i from 2 to sqrt(n), test if i² divides n. This focuses specifically on square divisors rather than general factorization.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Square-Free Check via Square Root Method
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to determine if a number has no prime factor with exponent ≥2 using square divisor testing up to √n.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that a number is square-free if and only if it's not divisible by any perfect square greater than 1. Instead of full prime factorization, we can directly test for square divisors. For any composite number n, if it has a repeated prime factor p (exponent ≥2), then p² will divide n, and p² ≤ n. By checking all i from 2 to √n and testing if i² divides n, we efficiently detect any square divisor. The algorithm: 1) Handle edge cases (n ≤ 1 returns false). 2) Iterate i from 2 to floor(√n). 3) For each i, check if n % (i*i) == 0. 4) If any such i exists, return false (not square-free). 5) Otherwise return true. Time complexity is O(√n) since we iterate up to √n. Space is O(1). Connection to L1: This uses the square divisor check as its core subroutine. Common mistakes: forgetting edge cases, using i ≤ √n instead of i² ≤ n (which is equivalent but clearer), or not handling large n efficiently. Real code pattern: def is_square_free(n): if n <= 1: return False; for i in range(2, int(n**0.5)+1): if n % (i*i) == 0: return False; return True.

examples.trace: n=18. √18≈4.24, floor=4. Check i=2: 2²=4, 18%4=2≠0. i=3: 3²=9, 18%9=0 → divisible by 9. Return False immediately.

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Check for Square Divisor
ladders.desc: Determine if a given number n is divisible by any perfect square up to a limit.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the square-free check. The intuition: to test if n has a repeated prime factor, we check if it's divisible by any perfect square i² where i ≥ 2. Since any repeated prime p implies p² divides n, and p ≤ √n, checking all i up to √n suffices. Algorithm: Input n. 1) Compute limit = floor(√n). 2) For i from 2 to limit: if n % (i*i) == 0, return true (has square divisor). 3) Return false. Time: O(√n), space O(1). Connection to L0: This directly implements the core test used in the full problem. Common mistakes: starting i from 1 (1²=1 divides everything, but doesn't indicate repeated primes), or incorrect limit calculation. Real code: def has_square_divisor(n): limit = int(n**0.5); for i in range(2, limit+1): if n % (i*i) == 0: return True; return False.

examples.trace: n=20. √20≈4.47, limit=4. i=2: 2²=4, 20%4=0 → divisible by 4. Return True.
n=15. √15≈3.87, limit=3. i=2: 2²=4, 15%4=3≠0. i=3: 3²=9, 15%9=6≠0. No divisor found, return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterate Up to Square Root
ladders.desc: Understand why checking up to √n is sufficient for divisor testing.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that for any divisor d of n, there exists a complementary divisor n/d. If d ≤ √n, then n/d ≥ √n. Thus, to find all divisors, we only need to check up to √n. For square divisors specifically: if n has a square divisor i², then i ≤ √n because i² ≤ n implies i ≤ √n. Therefore, iterating i from 2 to √n covers all possible i that could satisfy i² dividing n. Algorithm: Given n, set limit = floor(√n). Loop i from 2 to limit. This reduces checks from O(n) to O(√n). Time: O(√n), space O(1). Connection to L1: This iteration pattern is used in the square divisor check. Common mistakes: using i ≤ n instead of i ≤ √n (inefficient), or off-by-one errors in the loop range. Real code: limit = int(n**0.5); for i in range(2, limit+1): # perform check.

examples.trace: n=36. √36=6. limit=6. Loop i from 2 to 6 inclusive: i=2,3,4,5,6. Check each i²: 4,9,16,25,36.
n=10. √10≈3.16, floor=3. Loop i from 2 to 3: i=2,3. Check i²: 4,9.

testCases.expected: i values: (none, limit=1, loop doesn't run)

ladders.title: Building Block — Perfect Square Generation and Check
ladders.desc: Generate perfect squares and test divisibility.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves generating perfect squares (i² for integers i) and checking if they divide a given number n. The intuition: a perfect square is a number that can be expressed as k² for some integer k. For the square-free problem, we need to test if n is divisible by any such square where k ≥ 2. Algorithm: Input n. 1) Initialize i=2. 2) While i² ≤ n: compute square = i*i; check if n % square == 0; if yes, return true; else increment i. 3) Return false. Time: O(√n) as i grows until i² > n. Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This uses the square root limit (i² ≤ n) to control the loop. Common mistakes: using i ≤ n instead of i² ≤ n, or not handling integer overflow for large i. Real code: i=2; while i*i <= n: if n % (i*i) == 0: return True; i+=1; return False.

examples.trace: i=2: 2²=4 ≤20, check 20%4=0 → divisible. Stop early (return true).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Division and Modulo
ladders.desc: Perform division and remainder operations on integers.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves dividing two integers to check divisibility. The intuition: for integers a and b, a is divisible by b if a % b == 0, where % is the modulo operator giving remainder. In the square-free check, we use n % (i*i) == 0 to test if i² divides n. Algorithm: Given n and d (divisor), compute remainder = n % d. If remainder == 0, then d divides n evenly. Time: O(1) per operation. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used in the perfect square divisibility test. Common mistakes: confusing / (division) with % (modulo), or not handling division by zero. Real code: if n % d == 0: # divisible.

examples.trace: Compute 10 ÷ 2 = 5 remainder 0. Since remainder 0, 2 divides 10.
Compute 10 ÷ 3 = 3 remainder 1. Remainder 1 ≠ 0, so 3 does not divide 10.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Perfect Square?
ladders.desc: Understand the definition and examples of perfect squares.
ladders.explanation: A perfect square is a number that can be expressed as the square of an integer. Formally, for an integer k, k² is a perfect square. Examples: 1=1², 4=2², 9=3², 16=4², etc. The intuition: multiplying an integer by itself yields a square number. In the square-free problem, we care about perfect squares because if a number has a repeated prime factor, it will be divisible by some perfect square (the square of that prime). Algorithm to check if a number x is a perfect square: compute sqrt_x = √x, check if sqrt_x is an integer (e.g., sqrt_x == int(sqrt_x)). Time: O(1) with math functions. Space: O(1). Connection to L4: Perfect squares are the divisors we test for in the modulo operation. Common mistakes: confusing squares with other powers (e.g., cubes), or misidentifying non-squares. Real code: import math; def is_perfect_square(x): s = int(math.sqrt(x)); return s*s == x.

examples.trace: √9 = 3, which is an integer. 3*3 = 9, so 9 is a perfect square.

examples.output: False, because no integer squares to 10
examples.trace: √10 ≈ 3.16, not an integer. Check 3²=9, 4²=16, neither equals 10.

approaches.name: Pollard's Rho Algorithm O(n^(1/4))
approaches.summary: Use Pollard's Rho algorithm for integer factorization to find prime factors efficiently for large n. Check if any factor appears with exponent >= 2. This is much faster for very large integers.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Square-Free Check Using Pollard's Rho Factorization
ladders.desc: Complete implementation using Pollard's Rho algorithm to factor n and check if any prime factor has exponent ≥ 2.
ladders.explanation: This solution combines Pollard's Rho algorithm for efficient integer factorization with exponent checking to determine if a number is square-free. The intuition is that large numbers require faster factorization than trial division, and Pollard's Rho provides O(n^(1/4)) expected time by using random walks and cycle detection. First, we handle small factors with trial division up to 1000. Then for the remaining composite part, we repeatedly apply Pollard's Rho to find factors, recursively factor them, and store in a frequency map. Finally, we check if any factor appears more than once. The algorithm works because Pollard's Rho finds a non-trivial factor p by detecting a cycle in the sequence x_i = f(x_{i-1}) mod n, where f is a polynomial function, using Floyd's cycle-finding algorithm. Once a factor is found, we divide it out and continue. The space is O(log n) for recursion depth and factor storage. Connection to L1: This level integrates the Pollard's Rho subroutine from L1 with the overall factorization and exponent checking logic. Common mistakes include not handling small factors first (which slows down Pollard's Rho), incorrect cycle detection implementation, and not using Miller-Rabin for primality testing (Pollard's Rho only works on composite numbers). Real code pattern: Define helper functions for modular multiplication, Miller-Rabin primality test, Pollard's Rho factor finding, and recursive factorization, then check factor frequencies.

examples.trace: Step 1: Trial division up to 1000 finds factors 2, 3, 5 (all prime). Factor map: {2:1, 3:1, 5:1}. Step 2: No composite part remains. Step 3: Check exponents: all are 1, so square-free. Output true.

Step 1: Trial division finds 2 and 9. 2 is prime (exponent 1). 9 is composite. Step 2: Factor 9 using Pollard's Rho: f(x)=x^2+1 mod 9, start x=2, y=2. Iteration 1: x=f(2)=5, y=f(f(2))=f(5)=8. gcd(|5-8|,9)=gcd(3,9)=3, factor found. Step 3: Factor 3 from 9, now factor map: {2:1, 3:2}. Step 4: Check exponents: 3 appears twice, so not square-free. Output false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Pollard's Rho Algorithm for Factor Finding
ladders.desc: Implement Pollard's Rho algorithm to find a single non-trivial factor of a composite number n.
ladders.explanation: Pollard's Rho algorithm finds a factor p of n by detecting a cycle in a pseudo-random sequence modulo p. The intuition is that if we generate a sequence x_i = f(x_{i-1}) mod n where f is a polynomial (typically x^2 + c), then modulo a factor p of n, the sequence will eventually repeat (cycle) due to the pigeonhole principle. Floyd's cycle-finding algorithm uses two pointers (x and y) moving at different speeds to detect this cycle without storing the sequence. When x ≡ y mod p but x ≠ y mod n, gcd(|x-y|, n) reveals p. The algorithm runs in O(√p) expected time, which is O(n^(1/4)) for the smallest factor p. Steps: 1. If n is even, return 2. 2. Initialize x=2, y=2, d=1, c=random(1, n-1). 3. While d==1: x = (x*x + c) mod n, y = (y*y + c) mod n twice, d = gcd(|x-y|, n). 4. If d==n, restart with different c. Time: O(n^(1/4)) expected. Space: O(1). Connection to L0: This subroutine is called repeatedly in L0 to factor n. Common mistakes: not handling d==n (algorithm fails), poor choice of c, and integer overflow in modular multiplication. Real code pattern: Use a helper for modular multiplication, loop until factor found or max iterations.

examples.trace: Step 1: n odd, not even. Step 2: x=2, y=2, c=1. Step 3: Loop: x=(4+1) mod 15=5, y=((2^2+1)^2+1) mod 15: first y=5, then y=(25+1) mod 15=11. d=gcd(|5-11|,15)=gcd(6,15)=3. Factor 3 found.
Step 1: n odd. Step 2: x=2, y=2, c=1. Step 3: Loop: x=5, y: first 5, then 11. d=gcd(6,21)=3. Factor 3 found.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Cycle Detection with Floyd's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Use Floyd's cycle-finding algorithm to detect a cycle in a sequence without storing it.
ladders.explanation: Floyd's algorithm detects a cycle in a sequence by using two pointers that move at different speeds. The intuition is that if a sequence repeats, a fast pointer will eventually meet a slow pointer within the cycle. For Pollard's Rho, we generate sequence x_i = f(x_{i-1}) mod n. Let slow pointer x move one step per iteration: x = f(x). Let fast pointer y move two steps: y = f(f(y)). If there exists a factor p such that x ≡ y mod p, then gcd(|x-y|, n) may reveal p. This works because modulo p, the sequence is deterministic and finite, so a cycle must occur. The algorithm avoids storing the sequence, saving memory. Steps: Initialize x=y=seed. While true: x = f(x), y = f(f(y)), if gcd(|x-y|, n) > 1, return it. Time: O(cycle length) which is O(√p). Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This is the core of Pollard's Rho. Common mistakes: incorrect update of y (must apply f twice), and not using absolute value in gcd. Real code pattern: Loop with x and y updates, break when gcd > 1.

examples.trace: Step 1: x=2, y=2. Step 2: x=5, y: f(2)=5, f(5)=11, so y=11. gcd(|5-11|,15)=3 > 1, cycle detected modulo 3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Modular Arithmetic and Greatest Common Divisor (GCD)
ladders.desc: Compute GCD and perform modular multiplication efficiently.
ladders.explanation: Modular arithmetic and GCD are fundamental for Pollard's Rho. GCD is used to find common factors, and modular multiplication avoids overflow. The intuition: gcd(a,b) finds the largest number dividing both a and b; in Pollard's Rho, we compute gcd(|x-y|, n) to extract a factor of n. Euclidean algorithm computes GCD efficiently: gcd(a,b) = gcd(b, a mod b). For large numbers, we need modular multiplication (a*b mod n) without overflow by using binary multiplication or built-in big integers. Steps for GCD: While b != 0: (a, b) = (b, a mod b). Return a. For modular multiplication: Use (a * b) % n directly if language supports big integers, or implement with doubling. Time: O(log min(a,b)) for GCD, O(log n) for modular multiplication. Space: O(1). Connection to L2: GCD is used in cycle detection to find factors. Common mistakes: not handling negative numbers in GCD, and overflow in multiplication. Real code pattern: Define functions gcd(a,b) and mul_mod(a,b,n).

examples.trace: Step 1: a=48, b=18. Step 2: 48 mod 18 = 12, so a=18, b=12. Step 3: 18 mod 12 = 6, so a=12, b=6. Step 4: 12 mod 6 = 0, so a=6, b=0. Return 6.
Step 1: Compute 7*13 = 91. Step 2: 91 mod 30 = 1 (since 30*3=90, remainder 1). Output 1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Factorization by Trial Division
ladders.desc: Find all prime factors of a number by dividing by primes up to √n.
ladders.explanation: Trial division is the simplest factorization method: divide n by each integer from 2 to √n to find factors. The intuition: if n is composite, it has a factor ≤ √n. We repeatedly divide n by a factor until it's no longer divisible, recording the factor and its exponent. This is inefficient for large n (O(√n)) but useful for small factors in Pollard's Rho preprocessing. Steps: Initialize empty factor list. For i from 2 to √n: while n % i == 0: add i to list, n = n / i. If n > 1 after loop, add n (it's prime). Time: O(√n). Space: O(log n) for factors. Connection to L3: This uses modular arithmetic (division) and is a building block for full factorization. Common mistakes: not checking up to √n, and not handling repeated factors correctly. Real code pattern: Loop with while n%i==0 to count exponents.

examples.trace: Step 1: i=2, 28%2==0, add 2, n=14. 14%2==0, add 2, n=7. Step 2: i=3, 7%3!=0. i=4, skip (not prime, but algorithm checks all i). i=5,6 skip. i=7, 7%7==0, add 7, n=1. Output [2,2,7].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Numbers and Divisibility
ladders.desc: Understand prime numbers, divisibility, and basic number properties.
ladders.explanation: A prime number is an integer greater than 1 with no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. Divisibility: a divides b (a|b) if b % a == 0. Key intuition: Every integer > 1 can be uniquely factored into primes (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic). For square-free numbers, no prime factor repeats. Basic operations: Check if a number is prime by testing divisibility from 2 to √n. Check divisibility with modulo operator. This is foundational because factorization relies on breaking numbers into primes. Steps to check if p is prime: If p < 2, false. For i from 2 to √p: if p % i == 0, return false. Return true. Time: O(√p). Space: O(1). Connection to L4: This concept underlies trial division factorization. Common mistakes: forgetting to check numbers ≤ 1, and not optimizing to √n. Real code pattern: Loop with condition i*i <= n.

examples.trace: Step 1: 7 > 1. Step 2: i=2, 7%2=1 ≠0. i=3, 7%3=1 ≠0. i=4 > √7≈2.6, stop. No divisors found, so prime.
Step 1: 9 > 1. Step 2: i=2, 9%2=1 ≠0. i=3, 9%3=0, so divisible by 3, not prime.
